content.text:
Done. **PR #2 is open: https://github.com/briney/fold-at-scripps/pull/2**

- **Branch:** `docs/architecture` (pushed, tracking remote)
- **Commit:** `3a5de8b` ŌĆö "Add architecture document" (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, 222 lines)
- **PR #2** ŌåÆ base `main`, summarizing the key decisions, the four swappable boundaries, and the non-goals.

You now have a complete paper trail: **VISION** (merged) ŌåÆ **ARCHITECTURE** (in review). Once you merge #2, the natural next step is to turn this into an **implementation plan** ŌĆö decomposing the build into ordered, independently-buildable pieces (e.g. project scaffold ŌåÆ data model/migrations ŌåÆ service layer ŌåÆ Executor/scheduler ŌåÆ API ŌåÆ SPA ŌåÆ auth ŌåÆ packaging), each small enough to implement and test on its own.

Whenever you're ready for that, just say so and I'll start the planning pass. No rush ŌĆö and if you want to tweak the architecture doc first, I can revise before you merge.

One thing we haven't discussed from an architecture perspective is an admin console. We don't need anything crazy fancy at the start, but several things stand out as being necessary for a v1 product: 1) pre-appproval of email addresses as a prerequisite for account creation (or required post-creation approval by an admin) because we don't necessarily want anyone on the intranet to be able to create and account and provision resources; 2) change user quotas, or possibly switch between two levels of non-admin accounts: `standard` and a power user, which would allow us to easily provide/modify group-level permissions and/or quotas; 3) manage or initiate resetting of user passwords; 4) view and manage all jobs across all users (include jobs that have been "deleted" by users, which shouldn't actually delete the job but should instead just remove the job from the user's job history); etc. Any other obvious admin-level tasks that the v1 console should have?

Good catch ŌĆö the admin console is a genuine v1 gap, and it has real data-model implications we should settle before it goes into `ARCHITECTURE.md`. Let me validate your four, propose the additions I think are clearly v1, then surface the couple of real forks.

## Your four ŌĆö confirmed, with the shape each implies

1. **Account gating** ŌåÆ a `User.status` (`pending`/`active`/`disabled`) and/or an email allowlist/invite table. *(fork below ŌĆö which mechanism)*
2. **Quotas / tiers** ŌåÆ I'd split `role` (admin vs. not) from **`tier`** (`standard`/`power`), where a tier carries a default quota profile, **plus** per-user override fields. That gives you both group-level *and* individual control ŌĆö strictly more capable than either alone and barely more work, so I'd just do both rather than choose. *(confirm)*
3. **Password resets** ŌåÆ a reset-token mechanism; admin can initiate. *(fork below ŌĆö delivery)*
4. **Job oversight + soft-delete** ŌåÆ `Run.hidden_at` so a user "delete" only hides the run from their history; admin sees everything including hidden runs, and can cancel/kill running jobs and **hard-delete to reclaim storage**.

## Additional v1 capabilities I'd recommend

Each reuses the service layer and existing data ŌĆö no new infrastructure:

- **System / GPU status dashboard** ŌĆö the 8 cards (free/busy + what's running on each), queue depth and waits. Essential operational visibility for a shared node; it's how an admin answers "why is my job waiting?"
- **Usage reporting** ŌĆö per-user and system-wide GPU-hours over time. You're already recording GPU-hours; surfacing them *directly* serves the "usage tracking / fair shared resource" pillar and informs quota decisions.
- **Suspend/disable a user** ŌĆö block access while retaining data (offboarding, abuse); reuses the same `status` field as gating.
- **Admin role management + first-admin bootstrap** ŌĆö grant/revoke admin, and seed the first admin via env/CLI on deploy (non-optional: with gating, nobody can self-approve into existence).
- **Maintenance mode** ŌĆö pause job dispatch (let running jobs finish, hold the queue) for node maintenance or updating autobio images. Cheap and genuinely useful on a single node.
- **Tool catalog management** ŌĆö trigger the autobioŌåÆcatalog sync and enable/disable individual tools (e.g. hide a broken one).
- **Lightweight admin audit log** ŌĆö who approved/suspended/changed-quota/reset/cancelled. Cheap accountability for a multi-admin resource ŌĆö but the easiest thing to defer if you want v1 leaner.

**Defer past v1:** storage retention/auto-cleanup policies, permissions finer than the three levels, and richer dashboards.

**One architectural note:** the admin console isn't a separate system ŌĆö it's admin-scoped API endpoints + SPA admin views, all going through the **same service layer** (so quota edits, cancellations, etc. reuse the existing domain logic). This is the bespoke admin UI we knowingly accepted when we chose FastAPI over Django's free admin; it stays cheap precisely because it reuses the core.

Now the two real forks:

questions.question: How should account creation be gated?

options.label: Email allowlist / invite (pre-approval)
options.description: Admin pre-approves emails (or sends invites); only approved addresses can register. Gates at the door ŌĆö no account or resources provisioned before approval. Recommended.

options.label: Post-creation admin approval
options.description: Anyone on the intranet can register, but the account sits `pending` and can't submit jobs until an admin activates it. Catches self-serve users, but you manage a pending queue.

options.label: Both
options.description: Allowlist controls who can register, and admins retain activate/suspend control afterward. Most flexible, slightly more surface area.

questions.question: How should password resets work in v1? (This hinges on whether the intranet has a usable SMTP/mail server.)

options.label: Admin-mediated (no email dependency)
options.description: Admin initiates a reset; the system generates a one-time link or temporary password the admin shares out-of-band, and the user sets a new one. Works even with no mail server. Recommended for v1.

options.label: Self-service via email (requires SMTP)
options.description: User requests a reset and gets an emailed link. Better UX, but depends on a working intranet mail server.

options.label: Both
options.description: Self-service email reset for users, with admin-initiated reset as a fallback. Best UX, but needs SMTP and a bit more to build.

result.output: Your questions have been answered: "How should account creation be gated?"="Both", "How should password resets work in v1? (This hinges on whether the intranet has a usable SMTP/mail server.)"="Admin-mediated (no email dependency)". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

content.text:
Locked in:

- **Gating: Both** ŌĆö allowlist/invite controls who can register, *and* new accounts sit `pending` until an admin activates them (with `disabled` for later suspension).
- **Password reset: admin-mediated** ŌĆö one-time token/temp-password shared out-of-band, no SMTP dependency.
- **Quotas: tier profile (`standard`/`power`) + per-user override** (confirmed).
- Plus the additional v1 capabilities I proposed, including a lightweight audit log (the easy thing to drop later if you want).

Since PR #2 is still open and unmerged, the admin console belongs *in* the architecture doc ŌĆö so I'll fold all of this into `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` on the same `docs/architecture` branch. Making the edits now.

  orthogonal fields the admin console manages:

  - **`tier`** ŌĆö `standard` / `power`; maps to a default quota profile
    (config-defined), so quotas can be managed at the group level.
  - **`status`** ŌĆö `pending` / `active` / `disabled`. New accounts start
    `pending` until an admin activates them; `disabled` blocks access while
    retaining all data.
  Plus optional **per-user quota overrides** that take precedence over the tier
  profile. Everything scopes to a user.

input.new_string:
## Admin console

The admin console is **not a separate system** ŌĆö it is a set of admin-scoped API
endpoints plus admin views in the same SPA, all routed through the **same service
layer** so every admin action (activating a user, changing a quota, cancelling a
run) reuses the existing domain logic rather than poking the database directly.
Access is gated by `role = admin`. This is the bespoke admin UI we accepted when
choosing FastAPI over Django's built-in admin; it stays cheap because it reuses
the core.

**v1 capabilities:**

- **User management**
  - Maintain the registration **allowlist** / send invitations.
  - **Activate** `pending` accounts and **suspend / re-enable** (`disabled`)
    users.
  - Set a user's **tier** (`standard` / `power`) and **per-user quota
    overrides**.
  - **Initiate password resets** (mints a one-time token shared out-of-band).
  - **Grant / revoke admin** `role`. The first admin is seeded via env/CLI on
    deploy.
- **Job oversight** ŌĆö view **all** runs across users (including `hidden_at`
  runs), filter by user/status, **cancel/kill** running runs, and
  **hard-delete** runs to reclaim storage. (A user "delete" only hides; only an
  admin removes.)
- **System status** ŌĆö live view of the 8-GPU pool (free/busy and what is running
  on each), plus queue depth and waits.
- **Usage reporting** ŌĆö per-user and system-wide GPU-hours over time, to inform
  quota decisions and serve the "usage tracking / fair shared resource" goal.
- **Tool catalog management** ŌĆö trigger the autobio ŌåÆ catalog sync and
  enable/disable individual tools.
- **Maintenance mode** ŌĆö pause job dispatch (running jobs finish, the queue
  holds) for node maintenance or autobio image updates.
- **Audit log** ŌĆö record admin actions for accountability.

## The swappable boundaries

content.text:
Done ŌĆö the admin console is now folded into `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` on the `docs/architecture` branch (so it extends the open PR #2 rather than fragmenting the architecture across PRs). Changes:

- **Data model ŌĆö `User`** now carries `role` (admin access), `tier` (`standard`/`power` ŌåÆ quota profile), `status` (`pending`/`active`/`disabled`), and per-user quota overrides.
- **Data model ŌĆö `Run`** gains `hidden_at` (user "delete" = hide only; record + outputs retained).
- **New entities:** `AllowedEmail/Invitation`, `PasswordResetToken`, `AuditLog`, `SystemSettings` (holds `maintenance_mode`).
- **Quotas** paragraph updated for tier-profile + per-user override.
- **Job lifecycle** step 3 notes the scheduler honors `maintenance_mode`.
- **Authentication** notes gating (allowlist + `pending` activation) and first-admin bootstrap, linking to the new section.
- **New "Admin console" section** covering user management, job oversight, system/GPU status, usage reporting, tool-catalog management, maintenance mode, and the audit log ŌĆö explicitly framed as admin endpoints + SPA views over the same service layer.
- **Non-goals** expanded: storage retention/auto-cleanup, finer permissions, and self-service email/password flows.

Self-reviewed for consistency: no placeholders, the `#admin-console` cross-link resolves, and nothing contradicts the earlier sections.

input.command:
gh pr edit 2 --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

Adds `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` ŌĆö the technical architecture for **fold@Scripps**, the companion to `docs/VISION.md`. Where the vision states *why* and *what*, this states *how*. It records the structural decisions, the reasoning behind them, and the boundaries that keep the system simple now while leaving room to grow.

## Key decisions

- **Stack** ŌĆö FastAPI JSON backend + React/TypeScript/Tailwind SPA (Vite); PostgreSQL; model execution via the [`autobio`](https://github.com/briney/autobio) Python API.
- **Job queue / GPU** ŌĆö exclusive, variable-count whole-GPU allocation; a custom DB-backed scheduler behind an `Executor` interface (Postgres `SELECT ŌĆ” FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`, multi-node-reachable). No extra broker.
- **Process model** ŌĆö hybrid: nginx/API/Postgres in Docker Compose; the scheduler runs as a host systemd process with the Docker + GPU access it needs to launch autobio containers (the API needs neither).
- **Data model** ŌĆö User (role/tier/status + quota overrides), Tool catalog (schema-synced from autobio, version-pinned), Run (with `hidden_at` soft-delete), Artifact; per-run `inputs/config/outputs` dirs on local FS behind a `Storage` interface.
- **Quotas** ŌĆö enforce a per-user concurrency cap (tier profile + per-user override) in v1; record GPU-hours from day one so budgets can be switched on later without backfilling.
- **Auth** ŌĆö local accounts in v1 behind an identity-provider boundary; httpOnly cookie sessions; gated registration (allowlist + admin activation); per-user API tokens as the future programmatic credential.

## Admin console (v1)

Admin-scoped API endpoints + SPA views over the **same service layer** (not a separate system):

- **User management** ŌĆö registration allowlist/invites, activate/suspend, set tier + per-user quota overrides, admin-initiated password resets, grant/revoke admin (first admin seeded via env/CLI).
- **Job oversight** ŌĆö view all runs (incl. user-hidden), cancel/kill, hard-delete to reclaim storage.
- **System status** ŌĆö live 8-GPU pool view, queue depth/waits.
- **Usage reporting** ŌĆö per-user and system GPU-hours over time.
- **Tool catalog** ŌĆö trigger autobio sync, enable/disable tools.
- **Maintenance mode** ŌĆö pause dispatch for node/image maintenance.
- **Audit log** ŌĆö record admin actions.

## Growth, not walled off

Four swappable boundaries keep each growth axis reachable without building it now:

- **Service layer** (transport-agnostic core) ŌåÆ a programmatic API as another adapter
- **`Executor`** ŌåÆ multi-node / SLURM / k8s
- **`Storage`** ŌåÆ NFS / object storage
- **Identity provider** ŌåÆ SSO (OIDC/SAML) / LDAP

## Non-goals (for now)

Chained pipelines, multi-node scheduling, a public API, hardened auth, GPU packing/sharing, SSE/push status, storage retention/auto-cleanup, finer-grained permissions, and self-service email/password flows ŌĆö all reachable, none built in v1.
EOF
)"

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/briney/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/6.0.3/skills/writing-plans

# Writing Plans

## Overview

Write comprehensive implementation plans assuming the engineer has zero context for our codebase and questionable taste. Document everything they need to know: which files to touch for each task, code, testing, docs they might need to check, how to test it. Give them the whole plan as bite-sized tasks. DRY. YAGNI. TDD. Frequent commits.

Assume they are a skilled developer, but know almost nothing about our toolset or problem domain. Assume they don't know good test design very well.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the writing-plans skill to create the implementation plan."

**Context:** If working in an isolated worktree, it should have been created via the `superpowers:using-git-worktrees` skill at execution time.

**Save plans to:** `docs/superpowers/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<feature-name>.md`
- (User preferences for plan location override this default)

## Scope Check

If the spec covers multiple independent subsystems, it should have been broken into sub-project specs during brainstorming. If it wasn't, suggest breaking this into separate plans ŌĆö one per subsystem. Each plan should produce working, testable software on its own.

## File Structure

Before defining tasks, map out which files will be created or modified and what each one is responsible for. This is where decomposition decisions get locked in.

- Design units with clear boundaries and well-defined interfaces. Each file should have one clear responsibility.
- You reason best about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. Prefer smaller, focused files over large ones that do too much.
- Files that change together should live together. Split by responsibility, not by technical layer.
- In existing codebases, follow established patterns. If the codebase uses large files, don't unilaterally restructure - but if a file you're modifying has grown unwieldy, including a split in the plan is reasonable.

This structure informs the task decomposition. Each task should produce self-contained changes that make sense independently.

## Task Right-Sizing

A task is the smallest unit that carries its own test cycle and is worth a
fresh reviewer's gate. When drawing task boundaries: fold setup,
configuration, scaffolding, and documentation steps into the task whose
deliverable needs them; split only where a reviewer could meaningfully
reject one task while approving its neighbor. Each task ends with an
independently testable deliverable.

## Bite-Sized Task Granularity

**Each step is one action (2-5 minutes):**
- "Write the failing test" - step
- "Run it to make sure it fails" - step
- "Implement the minimal code to make the test pass" - step
- "Run the tests and make sure they pass" - step
- "Commit" - step

## Plan Document Header

**Every plan MUST start with this header:**

```markdown
# [Feature Name] Implementation Plan

> **For agentic workers:** REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (recommended) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task. Steps use checkbox (`- [ ]`) syntax for tracking.

**Goal:** [One sentence describing what this builds]

**Architecture:** [2-3 sentences about approach]

**Tech Stack:** [Key technologies/libraries]

## Global Constraints

[The spec's project-wide requirements ŌĆö version floors, dependency limits,
naming and copy rules, platform requirements ŌĆö one line each, with exact
values copied verbatim from the spec. Every task's requirements implicitly
include this section.]

---
```

## Task Structure

````markdown
### Task N: [Component Name]

**Files:**
- Create: `exact/path/to/file.py`
- Modify: `exact/path/to/existing.py:123-145`
- Test: `tests/exact/path/to/test.py`

**Interfaces:**
- Consumes: [what this task uses from earlier tasks ŌĆö exact signatures]
- Produces: [what later tasks rely on ŌĆö exact function names, parameter
  and return types. A task's implementer sees only their own task; this
  block is how they learn the names and types neighboring tasks use.]

- [ ] **Step 1: Write the failing test**

```python
def test_specific_behavior():
    result = function(input)
    assert result == expected
```

- [ ] **Step 2: Run test to verify it fails**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: FAIL with "function not defined"

- [ ] **Step 3: Write minimal implementation**

```python
def function(input):
    return expected
```

- [ ] **Step 4: Run test to verify it passes**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: PASS

- [ ] **Step 5: Commit**

```bash
git add tests/path/test.py src/path/file.py
git commit -m "feat: add specific feature"
```
````

## No Placeholders

Every step must contain the actual content an engineer needs. These are **plan failures** ŌĆö never write them:
- "TBD", "TODO", "implement later", "fill in details"
- "Add appropriate error handling" / "add validation" / "handle edge cases"
- "Write tests for the above" (without actual test code)
- "Similar to Task N" (repeat the code ŌĆö the engineer may be reading tasks out of order)
- Steps that describe what to do without showing how (code blocks required for code steps)
- References to types, functions, or methods not defined in any task

## Remember
- Exact file paths always
- Complete code in every step ŌĆö if a step changes code, show the code
- Exact commands with expected output
- DRY, YAGNI, TDD, frequent commits

## Self-Review

After writing the complete plan, look at the spec with fresh eyes and check the plan against it. This is a checklist you run yourself ŌĆö not a subagent dispatch.

**1. Spec coverage:** Skim each section/requirement in the spec. Can you point to a task that implements it? List any gaps.

**2. Placeholder scan:** Search your plan for red flags ŌĆö any of the patterns from the "No Placeholders" section above. Fix them.

**3. Type consistency:** Do the types, method signatures, and property names you used in later tasks match what you defined in earlier tasks? A function called `clearLayers()` in Task 3 but `clearFullLayers()` in Task 7 is a bug.

If you find issues, fix them inline. No need to re-review ŌĆö just fix and move on. If you find a spec requirement with no task, add the task.

## Execution Handoff

After saving the plan, offer execution choice:

**"Plan complete and saved to `docs/superpowers/plans/<filename>.md`. Two execution options:**

**1. Subagent-Driven (recommended)** - I dispatch a fresh subagent per task, review between tasks, fast iteration

**2. Inline Execution** - Execute tasks in this session using executing-plans, batch execution with checkpoints

**Which approach?"**

**If Subagent-Driven chosen:**
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development
- Fresh subagent per task + two-stage review

**If Inline Execution chosen:**
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:executing-plans
- Batch execution with checkpoints for review

I'm using the writing-plans skill to create the implementation plan.

## Scope check first

The architecture is a full multi-subsystem product ŌĆö backend scaffold, data model, auth, tool catalog, service layer, scheduler/executor, user API, admin API, an SPA, and deployment. That's far too much for one plan. The skill is explicit here: **decompose into a sequence of separate plans, each producing working, testable software on its own**, and write them one at a time (each gets its own plan ŌåÆ implement ŌåÆ review cycle).

So before I write anything, here's the proposed decomposition and build order. Each plan depends only on the ones before it, and each ends at an independently verifiable state:

| # | Plan | Deliverable (independently testable) |
|---|------|--------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Foundation & scaffold** | `src/` layout, `pyproject` (uv), FastAPI skeleton, Pydantic settings, Postgres + dev Docker Compose, pytest + ruff harness, health endpoint. App boots, health check + compose verified. |
| 2 | **Data model & migrations** | SQLAlchemy 2.0 models + Alembic for all entities (User, Tool, Run, Artifact, AllowedEmail, PasswordResetToken, AuditLog, SystemSettings). Migrations apply up/down; constraint tests pass. |
| 3 | **Auth & account lifecycle** | Password hashing, cookie sessions, identity-provider boundary, gated registration (allowlist + `pending`), login/logout, first-admin bootstrap CLI. |
| 4 | **Tool catalog** | autobioŌåÆcatalog sync (schema snapshot, version pinning), enable/disable. Catalog populates from a mocked autobio; queryable. |
| 5 | **Service layer, storage & run lifecycle** | `Storage` interface + local-FS impl, run creation (schema validation + quota enforcement at enqueue), state machine, soft-delete, `Executor` interface + **fake** executor. Full lifecycle tested without GPUs. |
| 6 | **Scheduler & autobio Executor** | Host scheduler loop, exclusive variable-count GPU allocation via `SKIP LOCKED`, real autobio Executor (Python API ŌåÆ Docker), GPU-seconds accounting, crash recovery, maintenance mode. |
| 7 | **User-facing API** | Routers over the service layer: catalog, run submit/list/get/cancel/soft-delete, ownership-checked downloads, usage, polling. |
| 8 | **Admin API** | Admin-scoped endpoints: user mgmt, job oversight, system/GPU status, usage reporting, catalog mgmt, maintenance mode, audit log. |
| 9 | **Frontend SPA** | React/TS/Tailwind/Vite: auth, tool browser, schema-driven guided forms, run dashboard + polling, downloads, admin views. |
| 10 | **Deployment & packaging** | Production Compose (nginx + API + Postgres), scheduler systemd unit, nginx static SPA + API proxy, env/secrets, ops docs. |

**Global constraints all plans will inherit** (from your CLAUDE.md + our decisions): Python 3.11+, `src/` layout, package `foldscripps`, `uv` for deps, `ruff` format/lint, `pytest` (TDD, real data where feasible), SQLAlchemy 2.0 + Alembic, Pydantic v2 / `BaseSettings`, FastAPI, type hints + Google docstrings, Ōēż100-char lines, frequent commits.

A couple could merge if you'd rather have fewer, larger plans (e.g. 7+8 into one "API" plan), but I'd keep them split ŌĆö cleaner review gates, and #6 and #9 are each big enough to deserve their own.

Once you confirm, I'll write Plan 1 to `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-06-30-foundation-scaffold.md`.

# Foundation & Scaffold Implementation Plan

> **For agentic workers:** REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (recommended) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task. Steps use checkbox (`- [ ]`) syntax for tracking.

**Goal:** Stand up the `fold_at_scripps` project skeleton ŌĆö a booting FastAPI app with typed settings, an async Postgres connection, liveness/readiness health checks, a dev Docker Compose stack, and a CI pipeline ŌĆö so every later plan builds on a tested foundation.

**Architecture:** A `src/`-layout Python package exposing a FastAPI app built by a factory function. Configuration is a Pydantic `BaseSettings` object. Database access is an async SQLAlchemy 2.0 engine/session, lazily created and injected via a FastAPI dependency. Health endpoints prove the app boots (`/health`) and can reach Postgres (`/health/ready`). Docker Compose runs the API + Postgres for local dev; GitHub Actions runs lint + tests against a Postgres service.

**Tech Stack:** Python 3.11, FastAPI, Uvicorn, Pydantic v2 + pydantic-settings, SQLAlchemy 2.0 (asyncio) + asyncpg, Postgres 16, pytest + pytest-asyncio + httpx, ruff, uv, Docker Compose, GitHub Actions.

## Global Constraints

- Python `>=3.11`; `target-version = "py311"`.
- `src/` layout; package name **`fold_at_scripps`**; distribution name `fold-at-scripps`.
- Dependency manager **`uv`**; commit `uv.lock`.
- Formatter/linter **`ruff`** (format + check); max line length **100**.
- Tests with **`pytest`** (TDD: failing test first). Async tests via `pytest-asyncio` with `asyncio_mode = "auto"`.
- Type hints on all signatures; `from __future__ import annotations` in every module; Google-style docstrings on public functions/classes.
- Absolute imports only; first-party package is `fold_at_scripps`.
- Settings come from the environment with prefix **`FOLD_`**; no secrets in code or committed config.
- Frequent commits ŌĆö one per task minimum, using the commit step.

---

### Task 1: Project scaffold, tooling, and settings

**Files:**
- Create: `pyproject.toml`
- Create: `src/fold_at_scripps/__init__.py`
- Create: `src/fold_at_scripps/config.py`
- Create: `tests/__init__.py`
- Create: `tests/conftest.py`
- Create: `tests/test_config.py`
- Modify: `.gitignore` (append Python entries)

**Interfaces:**
- Consumes: nothing (first task).
- Produces:
  - `fold_at_scripps.config.Settings` ŌĆö Pydantic settings with fields `app_name: str`, `debug: bool`, `database_url: str`; env prefix `FOLD_`.
  - `fold_at_scripps.config.get_settings() -> Settings` ŌĆö `lru_cache`d accessor exposing `.cache_clear()`.

- [ ] **Step 1: Create the package layout and `pyproject.toml`**

Create `src/fold_at_scripps/__init__.py`:

```python
"""fold@Scripps ŌĆö web front-end for running biological models via autobio."""

__version__ = "0.1.0"
```

Create `tests/__init__.py` (empty file).

Create `pyproject.toml`:

```toml
[project]
name = "fold-at-scripps"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "Web front-end for running biological models via autobio."
requires-python = ">=3.11"
dependencies = [
    "fastapi>=0.111",
    "uvicorn[standard]>=0.30",
    "pydantic>=2.7",
    "pydantic-settings>=2.3",
    "sqlalchemy[asyncio]>=2.0.30",
    "asyncpg>=0.29",
]

[dependency-groups]
dev = [
    "pytest>=8.2",
    "pytest-asyncio>=0.23",
    "httpx>=0.27",
    "ruff>=0.5",
]

[build-system]
requires = ["hatchling"]
build-backend = "hatchling.build"

[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]
packages = ["src/fold_at_scripps"]

[tool.ruff]
line-length = 100
target-version = "py311"

[tool.ruff.lint]
select = ["E", "F", "I", "UP", "B"]

[tool.ruff.lint.isort]
known-first-party = ["fold_at_scripps"]

[tool.pytest.ini_options]
asyncio_mode = "auto"
testpaths = ["tests"]
markers = [
    "integration: tests that require a running Postgres database",
]
```

- [ ] **Step 2: Append Python entries to `.gitignore`**

Append to the existing `.gitignore`:

```gitignore

# Python
__pycache__/
*.py[cod]
.venv/
*.egg-info/
.pytest_cache/
.ruff_cache/
.env
```

- [ ] **Step 3: Sync the environment**

Run: `uv sync`
Expected: creates `.venv/` and `uv.lock`, installs runtime + dev dependencies, and installs `fold_at_scripps` in editable mode. No errors.

- [ ] **Step 4: Write the failing settings test**

Create `tests/conftest.py`:

```python
"""Shared pytest fixtures."""

from __future__ import annotations

from collections.abc import Iterator

import pytest

from fold_at_scripps.config import get_settings


@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
def _clear_settings_cache() -> Iterator[None]:
    """Ensure each test sees freshly-loaded settings."""
    get_settings.cache_clear()
    yield
    get_settings.cache_clear()
```

Create `tests/test_config.py`:

```python
"""Tests for application settings."""

"""Application configuration loaded from the environment."""

from __future__ import annotations

from functools import lru_cache

from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict


class Settings(BaseSettings):
    """Environment-driven application settings (prefix ``FOLD_``)."""

    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(env_prefix="FOLD_", env_file=".env", extra="ignore")

    app_name: str = "fold@Scripps"
    debug: bool = False
    database_url: str = "REDACTED"


@lru_cache
def get_settings() -> Settings:
    """Return a process-wide cached :class:`Settings` instance."""
    return Settings()
```

Create `src/fold_at_scripps/db.py`:

```python
"""Async SQLAlchemy engine, session factory, and FastAPI dependency."""

from __future__ import annotations

from collections.abc import AsyncIterator

from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import (
    AsyncEngine,
    AsyncSession,
    async_sessionmaker,
    create_async_engine,
)

from fold_at_scripps.config import get_settings

_engine: AsyncEngine | None = None
_sessionmaker: async_sessionmaker[AsyncSession] | None = None


def get_engine() -> AsyncEngine:
    """Return the process-wide async engine, creating it on first use."""
    global _engine
    if _engine is None:
        _engine = create_async_engine(get_settings().database_url, pool_pre_ping=True)
    return _engine


def get_sessionmaker() -> async_sessionmaker[AsyncSession]:
    """Return the process-wide async session factory."""
    global _sessionmaker
    if _sessionmaker is None:
        _sessionmaker = async_sessionmaker(get_engine(), expire_on_commit=False)
    return _sessionmaker


async def get_session() -> AsyncIterator[AsyncSession]:
    """FastAPI dependency yielding an async database session."""
    async with get_sessionmaker()() as session:
        yield session
```

"""Health-check endpoints."""

from __future__ import annotations

from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends
from sqlalchemy import text
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession

@router.get("/health/ready")

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/briney/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/6.0.3/skills/subagent-driven-development

# Subagent-Driven Development

Execute plan by dispatching a fresh implementer subagent per task, a task review (spec compliance + code quality) after each, and a broad whole-branch review at the end.

**Why subagents:** You delegate tasks to specialized agents with isolated context. By precisely crafting their instructions and context, you ensure they stay focused and succeed at their task. They should never inherit your session's context or history ŌĆö you construct exactly what they need. This also preserves your own context for coordination work.

**Core principle:** Fresh subagent per task + task review (spec + quality) + broad final review = high quality, fast iteration

**Narration:** between tool calls, narrate at most one short line ŌĆö the
ledger and the tool results carry the record.

**Continuous execution:** Do not pause to check in with your human partner between tasks. Execute all tasks from the plan without stopping. The only reasons to stop are: BLOCKED status you cannot resolve, ambiguity that genuinely prevents progress, or all tasks complete. "Should I continue?" prompts and progress summaries waste their time ŌĆö they asked you to execute the plan, so execute it.

## When to Use

```dot
digraph when_to_use {
    "Have implementation plan?" [shape=diamond];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" [shape=diamond];
    "Stay in this session?" [shape=diamond];
    "subagent-driven-development" [shape=box];
    "executing-plans" [shape=box];
    "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [shape=box];

    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Tasks mostly independent?" [label="yes"];
    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no"];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Stay in this session?" [label="yes"];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no - tightly coupled"];
    "Stay in this session?" -> "subagent-driven-development" [label="yes"];
    "Stay in this session?" -> "executing-plans" [label="no - parallel session"];
}
```

**vs. Executing Plans (parallel session):**
- Same session (no context switch)
- Fresh subagent per task (no context pollution)
- Review after each task (spec compliance + code quality), broad review at the end
- Faster iteration (no human-in-loop between tasks)

## The Process

```dot
digraph process {
    rankdir=TB;

    subgraph cluster_per_task {
        label="Per Task";
        "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Implementer subagent asks questions?" [shape=diamond];
        "Answer questions, provide context" [shape=box];
        "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [shape=box];
        "Write diff file, dispatch task reviewer subagent (./task-reviewer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Task reviewer reports spec Ō£ģ and quality approved?" [shape=diamond];
        "Dispatch fix subagent for Critical/Important findings" [shape=box];
        "Mark task complete in todo list and progress ledger" [shape=box];
    }

    "Read plan, note context and global constraints, create todos" [shape=box];
    "More tasks remain?" [shape=diamond];
    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent (../requesting-code-review/code-reviewer.md)" [shape=box];
    "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch" [shape=box style=filled fillcolor=lightgreen];

    "Read plan, note context and global constraints, create todos" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" -> "Implementer subagent asks questions?";
    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Answer questions, provide context" [label="yes"];
    "Answer questions, provide context" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" -> "Write diff file, dispatch task reviewer subagent (./task-reviewer-prompt.md)";
    "Write diff file, dispatch task reviewer subagent (./task-reviewer-prompt.md)" -> "Task reviewer reports spec Ō£ģ and quality approved?";
    "Task reviewer reports spec Ō£ģ and quality approved?" -> "Dispatch fix subagent for Critical/Important findings" [label="no"];
    "Dispatch fix subagent for Critical/Important findings" -> "Write diff file, dispatch task reviewer subagent (./task-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="re-review"];
    "Task reviewer reports spec Ō£ģ and quality approved?" -> "Mark task complete in todo list and progress ledger" [label="yes"];
    "Mark task complete in todo list and progress ledger" -> "More tasks remain?";
    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [label="yes"];
    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent (../requesting-code-review/code-reviewer.md)" [label="no"];
    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent (../requesting-code-review/code-reviewer.md)" -> "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch";
}
```

## Pre-Flight Plan Review

Before dispatching Task 1, scan the plan once for conflicts:

- tasks that contradict each other or the plan's Global Constraints
- anything the plan explicitly mandates that the review rubric treats as a
  defect (a test that asserts nothing, verbatim duplication of a logic block)

Present everything you find to your human partner as one batched question ŌĆö
each finding beside the plan text that mandates it, asking which governs ŌĆö
before execution begins, not one interrupt per discovery mid-plan. If the
scan is clean, proceed without comment. The review loop remains the net for
conflicts that only emerge from implementation.

## Model Selection

Use the least powerful model that can handle each role to conserve cost and increase speed.

**Mechanical implementation tasks** (isolated functions, clear specs, 1-2 files): use a fast, cheap model. Most implementation tasks are mechanical when the plan is well-specified.

**Integration and judgment tasks** (multi-file coordination, pattern matching, debugging): use a standard model.

**Architecture and design tasks**: use the most capable available model.
The final whole-branch review is one of these ŌĆö dispatch it on the most
capable available model, not the session default.

**Review tasks**: choose the model with the same judgment, scaled to the
diff's size, complexity, and risk. A small mechanical diff does not need the
most capable model; a subtle concurrency change does.

**Always specify the model explicitly when dispatching a subagent.** An
omitted model inherits your session's model ŌĆö often the most capable and
most expensive ŌĆö which silently defeats this section.

**Turn count beats token price.** Wall-clock and context cost scale with how
many turns a subagent takes, and the cheapest models routinely take 2-3├Ś the
turns on multi-step work ŌĆö costing more overall. Use a mid-tier model as the
floor for reviewers and for implementers working from prose descriptions.
When the task's plan text contains the complete code to write, the
implementation is transcription plus testing: use the cheapest tier for
that implementer. Single-file mechanical fixes also take the cheapest tier.

**Task complexity signals (implementation tasks):**
- Touches 1-2 files with a complete spec ŌåÆ cheap model
- Touches multiple files with integration concerns ŌåÆ standard model
- Requires design judgment or broad codebase understanding ŌåÆ most capable model

## Handling Implementer Status

Implementer subagents report one of four statuses. Handle each appropriately:

**DONE:** Generate the review package (`scripts/review-package BASE HEAD`, from this skill's directory ŌĆö it prints the unique file path it wrote; BASE is the commit you recorded before dispatching the implementer ŌĆö never `HEAD~1`, which silently drops all but the last commit of a multi-commit task), then dispatch the task reviewer with the printed path.

**DONE_WITH_CONCERNS:** The implementer completed the work but flagged doubts. Read the concerns before proceeding. If the concerns are about correctness or scope, address them before review. If they're observations (e.g., "this file is getting large"), note them and proceed to review.

**NEEDS_CONTEXT:** The implementer needs information that wasn't provided. Provide the missing context and re-dispatch.

**BLOCKED:** The implementer cannot complete the task. Assess the blocker:
1. If it's a context problem, provide more context and re-dispatch with the same model
2. If the task requires more reasoning, re-dispatch with a more capable model
3. If the task is too large, break it into smaller pieces
4. If the plan itself is wrong, escalate to the human

**Never** ignore an escalation or force the same model to retry without changes. If the implementer said it's stuck, something needs to change.

## Handling Reviewer ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Items

The task reviewer may report "ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Cannot verify from diff" items ŌĆö requirements
that live in unchanged code or span tasks. These do not block the rest of the
review, but you must resolve each one yourself before marking the task
complete: you hold the plan and cross-task context the reviewer
lacks. If you confirm an item is a real gap, treat it as a failed spec
review ŌĆö send it back to the implementer and re-review.

## Constructing Reviewer Prompts

Per-task reviews are task-scoped gates. The broad review happens once, at the
final whole-branch review. When you fill a reviewer template:

- Do not add open-ended directives like "check all uses" or "run race tests
  if useful" without a concrete, task-specific reason
- Do not ask a reviewer to re-run tests the implementer already ran on the
  same code ŌĆö the implementer's report carries the test evidence
- Do not pre-judge findings for the reviewer ŌĆö never instruct a reviewer to
  ignore or not flag a specific issue. If you believe a finding would be a
  false positive, let the reviewer raise it and adjudicate it in the review
  loop. If the prompt you are writing contains "do not flag," "don't treat X
  as a defect," "at most Minor," or "the plan chose" ŌĆö stop: you are
  pre-judging, usually to spare yourself a review loop.
- The global-constraints block you hand the reviewer is its attention
  lens. Copy the binding requirements verbatim from the plan's Global
  Constraints section or the spec: exact values, exact formats, and the
  stated relationships between components ("same layout as X", "matches
  Y"). The reviewer's template already carries the process rules (YAGNI,
  test hygiene, review method) ŌĆö the constraints block is for what THIS
  project's spec demands.
- Hand the reviewer its diff as a file: run this skill's
  `scripts/review-package BASE HEAD` and pass the reviewer the file path
  it prints (or, without bash: `git log --oneline`, `git diff --stat`,
  and `git diff -U10` for the range, redirected to one uniquely named
  file). The output never enters your own context, and the reviewer sees
  the commit list, stat summary, and full diff with context in one Read
  call. Use the BASE you recorded before dispatching the implementer ŌĆö
  never `HEAD~1`, which silently truncates multi-commit tasks.
- A dispatch prompt describes one task, not the session's history. Do not
  paste accumulated prior-task summaries ("state after Tasks 1-3") into
  later dispatches ŌĆö a real session's dispatch hit 42k chars of which 99%
  was pasted history. A fresh subagent needs its task, the interfaces it
  touches, and the global constraints. Nothing else.
- Dispatch fix subagents for Critical and Important findings. Record Minor
  findings in the progress ledger as you go, and point the final
  whole-branch review at that list so it can triage which must be fixed
  before merge. A roll-up nobody reads is a silent discard.
- A finding labeled plan-mandated ŌĆö or any finding that conflicts with
  what the plan's text requires ŌĆö is the human's decision, like any plan
  contradiction: present the finding and the plan text, ask which governs.
  Do not dismiss the finding because the plan mandates it, and do not
  dispatch a fix that contradicts the plan without asking.
- The final whole-branch review gets a package too: run
  `scripts/review-package MERGE_BASE HEAD` (MERGE_BASE = the commit the
  branch started from, e.g. `git merge-base main HEAD`) and include the
  printed path in the final review dispatch, so the final reviewer reads
  one file instead of re-deriving the branch diff with git commands.
- Every fix dispatch carries the implementer contract: the fix subagent
  re-runs the tests covering its change and reports the results. Name the
  covering test files in the dispatch ŌĆö a one-line fix does not need the
  whole suite. Before re-dispatching the reviewer, confirm the fix report
  contains the covering tests, the command run, and the output; dispatch
  the re-review once all three are present.
- If the final whole-branch review returns findings, dispatch ONE fix
  subagent with the complete findings list ŌĆö not one fixer per finding.
  Per-finding fixers each rebuild context and re-run suites; a real
  session's final-review fix wave cost more than all its tasks combined.

## File Handoffs

Everything you paste into a dispatch prompt ŌĆö and everything a subagent
prints back ŌĆö stays resident in your context for the rest of the session
and is re-read on every later turn. Hand artifacts over as files:

- **Task brief:** before dispatching an implementer, run this skill's
  `scripts/task-brief PLAN_FILE N` ŌĆö it extracts the task's full text to a
  uniquely named file and prints the path. Compose the dispatch so the
  brief stays the single source of requirements. Your dispatch should
  contain: (1) one line on where this task fits in the project; (2) the
  brief path, introduced as "read this first ŌĆö it is your requirements,
  with the exact values to use verbatim"; (3) interfaces and decisions
  from earlier tasks that the brief cannot know; (4) your resolution of
  any ambiguity you noticed in the brief; (5) the report-file path and
  report contract. Exact values (numbers, magic strings, signatures, test
  cases) appear only in the brief.
- **Report file:** name the implementer's report file after the brief
  (brief `ŌĆ”/task-N-brief.md` ŌåÆ report `ŌĆ”/task-N-report.md`) and put it in
  the dispatch prompt. The implementer writes the full report there and
  returns only status, commits, a one-line test summary, and concerns.
- **Reviewer inputs:** the task reviewer gets three paths ŌĆö the same brief
  file, the report file, and the review package ŌĆö plus the global
  constraints that bind the task.
- Fix dispatches append their fix report (with test results) to the same
  report file and return a short summary; re-reviews read the updated file.

## Durable Progress

Conversation memory does not survive compaction. In real sessions,
controllers that lost their place have re-dispatched entire completed task
sequences ŌĆö the single most expensive failure observed. Track progress in
a ledger file, not only in todos.

- At skill start, check for a ledger:
  `cat "$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)/.superpowers/sdd/progress.md"`. Tasks listed there
  as complete are DONE ŌĆö do not re-dispatch them; resume at the first task
  not marked complete.
- When a task's review comes back clean, append one line to the ledger in
  the same message as your other bookkeeping:
  `Task N: complete (commits <base7>..<head7>, review clean)`.
- The ledger is your recovery map: the commits it names exist in git even
  when your context no longer remembers creating them. After compaction,
  trust the ledger and `git log` over your own recollection.
- `git clean -fdx` will destroy the ledger (it's git-ignored scratch); if
  that happens, recover from `git log`.

## Prompt Templates

- [implementer-prompt.md](implementer-prompt.md) - Dispatch implementer subagent
- [task-reviewer-prompt.md](task-reviewer-prompt.md) - Dispatch task reviewer subagent (spec compliance + code quality)
- Final whole-branch review: use superpowers:requesting-code-review's [code-reviewer.md](../requesting-code-review/code-reviewer.md)

## Example Workflow

```
You: I'm using Subagent-Driven Development to execute this plan.

[Read plan file once: docs/superpowers/plans/feature-plan.md]
[Create todos for all tasks]

Task 1: Hook installation script

[Run task-brief for Task 1; dispatch implementer with brief + report paths + context]

Implementer: "Before I begin - should the hook be installed at user or system level?"

You: "User level (~/.config/superpowers/hooks/)"

Implementer: "Got it. Implementing now..."
[Later] Implementer:
  - Implemented install-hook command
  - Added tests, 5/5 passing
  - Self-review: Found I missed --force flag, added it
  - Committed

[Run review-package, dispatch task reviewer with the printed path]
Task reviewer: Spec Ō£ģ - all requirements met, nothing extra.
  Strengths: Good test coverage, clean. Issues: None. Task quality: Approved.

[Mark Task 1 complete]

Task 2: Recovery modes

[Run task-brief for Task 2; dispatch implementer with brief + report paths + context]

Implementer: [No questions, proceeds]
Implementer:
  - Added verify/repair modes
  - 8/8 tests passing
  - Self-review: All good
  - Committed

[Run review-package, dispatch task reviewer with the printed path]
Task reviewer: Spec ŌØī:
  - Missing: Progress reporting (spec says "report every 100 items")
  - Extra: Added --json flag (not requested)
  Issues (Important): Magic number (100)

[Dispatch fix subagent with all findings]
Fixer: Removed --json flag, added progress reporting, extracted PROGRESS_INTERVAL constant

[Task reviewer reviews again]
Task reviewer: Spec Ō£ģ. Task quality: Approved.

[Mark Task 2 complete]

...

[After all tasks]
[Dispatch final code-reviewer]
Final reviewer: All requirements met, ready to merge

Done!
```

## Advantages

**vs. Manual execution:**
- Subagents follow TDD naturally
- Fresh context per task (no confusion)
- Parallel-safe (subagents don't interfere)
- Subagent can ask questions (before AND during work)

**vs. Executing Plans:**
- Same session (no handoff)
- Continuous progress (no waiting)
- Review checkpoints automatic

**Efficiency gains:**
- Controller curates exactly what context is needed; bulk artifacts move
  as files, not pasted text
- Subagent gets complete information upfront
- Questions surfaced before work begins (not after)

**Quality gates:**
- Self-review catches issues before handoff
- Task review carries two verdicts: spec compliance and code quality
- Review loops ensure fixes actually work
- Spec compliance prevents over/under-building
- Code quality ensures implementation is well-built

**Cost:**
- More subagent invocations (implementer + reviewer per task)
- Controller does more prep work (extracting all tasks upfront)
- Review loops add iterations
- But catches issues early (cheaper than debugging later)

## Red Flags

**Never:**
- Start implementation on main/master branch without explicit user consent
- Skip task review, or accept a report missing either verdict (spec compliance AND task quality are both required)
- Proceed with unfixed issues
- Dispatch multiple implementation subagents in parallel (conflicts)
- Make a subagent read the whole plan file (hand it its task brief ŌĆö
  `scripts/task-brief` ŌĆö instead)
- Skip scene-setting context (subagent needs to understand where task fits)
- Ignore subagent questions (answer before letting them proceed)
- Accept "close enough" on spec compliance (reviewer found spec issues = not done)
- Skip review loops (reviewer found issues = implementer fixes = review again)
- Let implementer self-review replace actual review (both are needed)
- Tell a reviewer what not to flag, or pre-rate a finding's severity in the
  dispatch prompt ("treat it as Minor at most") ŌĆö the plan's example code is
  a starting point, not evidence that its weaknesses were chosen
- Dispatch a task reviewer without a diff file ŌĆö generate it first
  (`scripts/review-package BASE HEAD`) and name the printed path in the
  prompt
- Move to next task while the review has open Critical/Important issues
- Re-dispatch a task the progress ledger already marks complete ŌĆö check
  the ledger (and `git log`) after any compaction or resume

**If subagent asks questions:**
- Answer clearly and completely
- Provide additional context if needed
- Don't rush them into implementation

**If reviewer finds issues:**
- Implementer (same subagent) fixes them
- Reviewer reviews again
- Repeat until approved
- Don't skip the re-review

**If subagent fails task:**
- Dispatch fix subagent with specific instructions
- Don't try to fix manually (context pollution)

## Integration

**Required workflow skills:**
- **superpowers:using-git-worktrees** - Ensures isolated workspace (creates one or verifies existing)
- **superpowers:writing-plans** - Creates the plan this skill executes
- **superpowers:requesting-code-review** - Code review template for the final whole-branch review
- **superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch** - Complete development after all tasks

**Subagents should use:**
- **superpowers:test-driven-development** - Subagents follow TDD for each task

**Alternative workflow:**
- **superpowers:executing-plans** - Use for parallel session instead of same-session execution

input.prompt:
You are implementing Task 1: Project scaffold, tooling, and settings.

## Task Description

Read your task brief first ŌĆö it is your requirements, with the exact values (file contents, config, test code) to use verbatim:
/home/briney/git/fold-at-scripps/.superpowers/sdd/task-1-brief.md

## Context

This is the first task of the "Foundation & Scaffold" plan for **fold@Scripps**, a FastAPI web application that serves as a multi-user front-end for running biological models via the `autobio` package. This task establishes the project skeleton: `src/` layout, `pyproject.toml` (managed by `uv`), ruff/pytest configuration, and a typed `Settings` module.

Current repo state: only `LICENSE`, `README.md`, `docs/`, and an existing `.gitignore` (which already contains agent/tool-config directory entries). You are on branch `feature/foundation-scaffold`. Later tasks build the FastAPI app, DB layer, Docker Compose, and CI on top of this, so the names you produce (`Settings`, `get_settings`) must match the brief exactly.

Work from: /home/briney/git/fold-at-scripps

## Global Constraints (apply to all work)

- Python >=3.11; ruff target-version py311.
- `src/` layout; package name `fold_at_scripps`; distribution name `fold-at-scripps`.
- Dependency manager `uv`; commit `uv.lock`.
- ruff format + check; max line length 100.
- pytest, TDD (failing test first). Async tests via pytest-asyncio with asyncio_mode = "auto".
- Type hints on all signatures; `from __future__ import annotations` in every module; Google-style docstrings on public functions/classes.
- Absolute imports only; first-party package `fold_at_scripps`.
- Settings from environment with prefix `FOLD_`; no secrets in code.

## Interfaces

- Consumes: nothing (first task).
- Produces (later tasks depend on these exact names): `fold_at_scripps.config.Settings` (fields `app_name: str`, `debug: bool`, `database_url: str`) and `fold_at_scripps.config.get_settings() -> Settings` (an `lru_cache`d accessor).

## Ambiguity resolution

## What Was Requested

- `src/` layout; package name `fold_at_scripps`; distribution name `fold-at-scripps`.
- Dependency manager `uv`; `uv.lock` committed.
- ruff format + check; max line length 100.
- pytest, TDD (failing test first). Async tests via pytest-asyncio with asyncio_mode = "auto".
- Type hints on all signatures; `from __future__ import annotations` in every module; Google-style docstrings on public functions/classes.
- Absolute imports only; first-party package `fold_at_scripps`.
- Settings from environment with prefix `FOLD_`; no secrets in code.

## What the Implementer Claims They Built

Read the implementer's report: /home/briney/git/fold-at-scripps/.superpowers/sdd/task-1-report.md

## Diff Under Review

**Base:** a5158fb
**Head:** 236f853
**Diff file:** /home/briney/git/fold-at-scripps/.superpowers/sdd/review-a5158fb..236f853.diff

Read the diff file once ŌĆö it contains the commit list, a stat summary, and the full diff with surrounding context. Factual note: the bulk of the byte count is the generated `uv.lock`; the hand-written files are `pyproject.toml`, `src/fold_at_scripps/__init__.py`, `src/fold_at_scripps/config.py`, `tests/conftest.py`, `tests/test_config.py`, and the `.gitignore` append. The diff's context lines ARE the changed files: do not Read a changed file separately unless a hunk you must judge is cut off mid-function. Do not re-run git commands. Do not crawl the broader codebase.

Your review is read-only on this checkout. Do not mutate the working tree, index, HEAD, or branch state.

## Do Not Trust the Report

Treat the implementer's report as unverified claims. Verify against the diff. A stated rationale never downgrades a finding's severity.

## Tests

The implementer already ran the tests with TDD evidence for this code. Do not re-run the suite to confirm their report. Run a focused test only if reading the code raises a specific doubt no existing run answers; otherwise recommend it. Warnings/noise in reported test output are findings ŌĆö output should be pristine.

## Part 1: Spec Compliance

Compare the diff against What Was Requested ŌĆö Missing / Extra / Misunderstood. If a requirement can't be verified from this diff alone, report it as a ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ item.

## Part 2: Code Quality

Clean separation; proper error handling; DRY without premature abstraction; edge cases. Tests verify real behavior (not mocks); edge cases covered. Each file one clear responsibility; follows the plan's file structure; this change doesn't create already-large files. Cite file:line for every finding.

Your final message is the report itself: begin directly with the spec-compliance verdict. Every line is a verdict, a finding with file:line, or a check you ran ŌĆö no preamble, no closing summary.

## Calibration

Categorize by actual severity. Important = task cannot be trusted until fixed. "Coverage could be broader" and polish are Minor. If the brief explicitly mandates something this rubric calls a defect, report it as Important labeled plan-mandated. Acknowledge what was done well before listing issues.

### Strengths
[specific]

For each: file:line, what's wrong, why it matters, how to fix.

**Reasoning:** [1-2 sentences]

---

### Spec Compliance

- Ō£ģ `pyproject.toml` ŌĆö matches brief exactly: `name = "fold-at-scripps"`, `version = "0.1.0"`, `requires-python = ">=3.11"`, all six runtime deps at specified minimum versions, all four dev deps at specified minimum versions, hatchling build backend, `packages = ["src/fold_at_scripps"]`, ruff `line-length = 100`, `target-version = "py311"`, ruff lint selects `["E", "F", "I", "UP", "B"]`, isort `known-first-party = ["fold_at_scripps"]`, pytest `asyncio_mode = "auto"`, `testpaths = ["tests"]`, integration marker ŌĆö every field is present and correct.
- Ō£ģ `src/fold_at_scripps/__init__.py` ŌĆö docstring and `__version__ = "0.1.0"` match brief verbatim.
- Ō£ģ `src/fold_at_scripps/config.py` ŌĆö `Settings(BaseSettings)` with `env_prefix="FOLD_"`, `env_file=".env"`, `extra="ignore"`, fields `app_name`, `debug`, `database_url` with correct types and defaults, `@lru_cache` on `get_settings()`, `from __future__ import annotations`, Google-style docstrings present.
- Ō£ģ `tests/__init__.py` ŌĆö empty file, present.
- Ō£ģ `tests/conftest.py` ŌĆö `autouse` fixture `_clear_settings_cache`, `cache_clear()` called before and after, `from __future__ import annotations`, correct `Iterator[None]` return type.
- Ō£ģ `tests/test_config.py` ŌĆö `test_settings_defaults` and `test_settings_read_from_env` match brief exactly; `from __future__ import annotations` present; type hints on signatures.
- Ō£ģ `.gitignore` ŌĆö appended the seven Python entries (`__pycache__/`, `*.py[cod]`, `.venv/`, `*.egg-info/`, `.pytest_cache/`, `.ruff_cache/`, `.env`) after existing entries, not overwriting.
- Ō£ģ `uv.lock` ŌĆö committed; `uv sync` was run.
- Ō£ģ TDD sequence: RED phase demonstrated `ModuleNotFoundError`, GREEN phase shows 2 passed, lint step shows `All checks passed!`.
- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Cannot verify from diff: `from __future__ import annotations` is absent from `src/fold_at_scripps/__init__.py` ŌĆö the brief does not show it there either (the brief's snippet for `__init__.py` also omits it), so this is not a defect per the brief. Acceptable.
- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Cannot verify from diff: whether `.venv/` is present but untracked (expected behavior from `uv sync`). Not a code defect; just unverifiable from static diff.

---

### Strengths

- Implementation matches the brief with zero drift ŌĆö every field, default value, decorator style (`@lru_cache` not `@lru_cache(maxsize=None)`), and docstring matches the spec's code blocks character-for-character.
- `_clear_settings_cache` fixture uses `autouse=True` and clears both before and after each test, making `test_settings_read_from_env` reliable against test ordering and residual cache state.
- `extra="ignore"` on `SettingsConfigDict` is a correct defensive choice ŌĆö prevents pydantic-settings from raising on unknown `FOLD_*` env vars introduced by future tasks.
- The `Iterator[None]` return type annotation on the fixture is correct and precise (not `Generator`).
- Absolute imports only; `from __future__ import annotations` present in all three Python modules that have function signatures.
- `uv.lock` committed as required; `requires-python = ">=3.11"` is propagated into the lock header.

---

### Issues

#### Critical (Must Fix)

None.

#### Important (Should Fix)

**`tests/conftest.py:11` ŌĆö fixture return type annotation is technically incorrect for an async-aware test suite.**
The fixture is typed `-> Iterator[None]` and is synchronous, which is correct for a sync fixture. However, the global constraint mandates `asyncio_mode = "auto"`, which means future async fixtures will use `AsyncIterator[None]`. This fixture itself is fine as written ŌĆö it does not need to be async. No action required on this fixture; flagged only as a reminder that async teardown fixtures added later must use `AsyncIterator[None]` / `AsyncGenerator[None, None]`. Not a defect in this task.

#### Minor (Nice to Have)

**`tests/test_config.py:14` ŌĆö `test_settings_defaults` does not assert `database_url`.**
The spec's `Settings` interface specifies `database_url: str` as a required field. The test for defaults checks `app_name` and `debug` but skips asserting the default value of `database_url` (`"REDACTED"`). The behavior is implicitly tested by `test_settings_read_from_env`, but a direct assertion in `test_settings_defaults` would make the default more explicit and prevent silent regression if the default ever changes. Add `assert settings.database_url == "REDACTED"` to `test_settings_defaults`. This is not mandated by the brief's test code, so it is Minor.

**`pyproject.toml:88ŌĆō92` ŌĆö no `addopts` or `filterwarnings` in `[tool.pytest.ini_options]`.**
The test report output is described as "pristine," but `pytest-asyncio>=0.23` is known to emit `PytestUnraisableExceptionWarning` and deprecation notices in some configurations when `asyncio_mode = "auto"` is active and no `filterwarnings` block is set. This is speculative ŌĆö the GREEN phase output shown in the report is clean ŌĆö but it is worth adding `filterwarnings = ["error"]` once the suite grows, to catch warnings-as-errors early. Minor cosmetic/future-proofing concern; not a defect for this task.

---

### Assessment

**Task quality:** Approved

**Reasoning:** All specified files are present and match the brief verbatim, the TDD cycle is properly documented with a clean REDŌåÆGREEN transition, ruff reports no issues, and the test design (autouse cache-clearing fixture + monkeypatch) is sound. The only gap is a missing assertion on `database_url`'s default value in `test_settings_defaults`, which is minor and not mandated by the brief's provided test code.</result>

You are implementing Task 2: FastAPI app factory and liveness health check.

## Task Description

Read your task brief first ŌĆö it is your requirements, with the exact values (file contents, test code) to use verbatim:
/home/briney/git/fold-at-scripps/.superpowers/sdd/task-2-brief.md

## Context

This is Task 2 of the "Foundation & Scaffold" plan for **fold@Scripps**, a FastAPI front-end for the `autobio` biological-models package. Task 1 (already complete on this branch) scaffolded the project: `src/fold_at_scripps/` with `__init__.py` and `config.py` (exposing `Settings` and `get_settings()`), `pyproject.toml` (uv), ruff/pytest config, and `tests/` with `conftest.py` (an autouse `_clear_settings_cache` fixture) and `test_config.py`. The environment is already synced (`uv sync` done, `.venv/` present).

This task adds the FastAPI application factory and a liveness health endpoint. Task 4 will later add a readiness endpoint to the same health router and a DB layer, so keep the health router importable and the names exactly as the brief specifies.

Work from: /home/briney/git/fold-at-scripps

## Global Constraints (apply to all work)

- Python >=3.11; ruff target-version py311.

## Task Description

/home/briney/git/fold-at-scripps/.superpowers/sdd/task-4-brief.md

## Context

This is Task 4 of the "Foundation & Scaffold" plan for **fold@Scripps**, a FastAPI front-end for the `autobio` biological-models package. Prior tasks (complete on this branch) produced: `config.py` (`Settings` with a `database_url` field defaulting to `REDACTED`, and `get_settings()`); `main.py` (`create_app()`, `app`); `api/health.py` (router with `GET /health` ŌåÆ `{"status":"ok"}`); tests with an autouse `_clear_settings_cache` fixture in `tests/conftest.py`; and a `docker-compose.yml` whose `postgres` service exposes Postgres on `localhost:5432` (db `fold_at_scripps`, user/pw `fold`/`fold`).

This task adds `src/fold_at_scripps/db.py` (async SQLAlchemy engine, sessionmaker, and a `get_session` FastAPI dependency), adds a `GET /health/ready` readiness endpoint to the health router that runs `SELECT 1`, and adds integration tests. Integration tests are marked `@pytest.mark.integration` and require a running Postgres.

Work from: /home/briney/git/fold-at-scripps

## Global Constraints

- Python >=3.11; package `fold_at_scripps`; `uv` for commands.
- ruff format + check; max line length 100.
- pytest, TDD (failing test first). asyncio_mode = "auto" is configured; integration tests use the `integration` marker.
- Type hints on all signatures; `from __future__ import annotations` in every module; Google-style docstrings on public functions/classes.
- Absolute imports only.

## Interfaces

- Consumes (from Task 1): `fold_at_scripps.config.get_settings` (for `database_url`).
- Produces (later tasks depend on these exact names): `fold_at_scripps.db.get_engine() -> AsyncEngine`, `fold_at_scripps.db.get_sessionmaker() -> async_sessionmaker[AsyncSession]`, `fold_at_scripps.db.get_session() -> AsyncIterator[AsyncSession]` (FastAPI dependency); and `GET /health/ready` returning `{"status": "ready"}` when the DB is reachable.

## Postgres for tests

Docker is available. Before running the integration tests, start Postgres: `docker compose up -d postgres` (wait until it is healthy ŌĆö `docker compose ps` shows `healthy`). The integration tests connect to `localhost:5432` using the `Settings` default `database_url`. Note: Task 3's brief mentioned a pre-existing unrelated container may use port 8000 ŌĆö that does not affect Postgres on 5432, but if `docker compose up -d postgres` reports a port conflict on 5432, report it as a concern rather than altering unrelated containers.

## Git

- Commit on the current branch `feature/foundation-scaffold`. Do NOT create new branches and do NOT push.

## Before You Begin

If anything in the brief is unclear, ask now.

## Your Job

1. Implement exactly what the brief specifies, following its TDD steps in order (each: write failing test ŌåÆ run to confirm it fails for the expected reason ŌåÆ implement ŌåÆ run to confirm pass). Note the brief replaces the entire contents of `api/health.py` in one step ŌĆö apply that replacement as written (it re-states the liveness endpoint plus the new readiness endpoint).
2. Run focused tests while iterating; run the full suite once before committing (integration tests need Postgres up).
3. Commit as the brief's commit step directs.
4. Self-review (completeness, quality, YAGNI, pristine test output).
5. Report back.

## Report Format

Write your full report to /home/briney/git/fold-at-scripps/.superpowers/sdd/task-4-report.md, including: what you implemented, what you tested and results, TDD evidence (RED command + failing output + why expected; GREEN command + passing output) for both the db test and the readiness test, files changed, self-review findings, any concerns.

- One-line test summary (e.g. "5/5 passing incl. 2 integration, output pristine")
- Concerns, if any
- The report file path

If BLOCKED or NEEDS_CONTEXT, put the specifics in your final message.

### Spec Compliance

- Ō£ģ `src/fold_at_scripps/db.py` created with exact interfaces: `get_engine()`, `get_sessionmaker()`, `get_session()` ŌĆö correct names, signatures, and return types.

- ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Cannot verify from diff: `asyncio_mode = "auto"` is set in `pyproject.toml` ŌĆö the async tests run without explicit `@pytest.mark.asyncio`, which implies it is set, but `pyproject.toml` is not in the diff. The integration tests passing confirms it indirectly.

---

### Strengths

- `db.py` is a verbatim match to the spec's implementation, with no gold-plating: lazy singletons, `pool_pre_ping=True`, `expire_on_commit=False`, and a clean generator-based `get_session`.
- `health.py` is a clean diff ŌĆö only what's needed is added; the liveness handler is untouched.
- The `_reset_db_singletons` fixture is well-documented with a precise, technically accurate docstring explaining the event-loop binding problem. The fixture is minimal: two null assignments before and after, nothing else.
- All files carry `from __future__ import annotations`, type hints on every signature, and Google-style docstrings. No wildcard imports, no `os.path`, no bare `except`.
- The `_clear_settings_cache` pattern from the existing conftest is correctly preserved; the new fixture follows the same structure.

---

### Issues

#### Critical (Must Fix)

None.

#### Important (Should Fix)

**`src/fold_at_scripps/api/health.py:41` ŌĆö `# noqa: B008` is missing the required explanatory comment.**

```python
async def readiness(session: AsyncSession = Depends(get_session)) -> dict[str, str]:  # noqa: B008
```

The project rule states: *"do not add `# type: ignore`/suppressions without a specific error code AND a comment explaining why."* The suppression carries `B008` (the specific error code ŌĆö correct), but has no inline explanation of why. The report's rationale ("canonical FastAPI dependency injection pattern") belongs on the line, not only in the report. The fix is one-line:

```python
async def readiness(session: AsyncSession = Depends(get_session)) -> dict[str, str]:  # noqa: B008 ŌĆö FastAPI Depends() in a default arg is the canonical DI pattern; B008 is a false positive here
```

This is an explicit project constraint, not a style preference. The suppression is technically correct and minimal; the error code is present; only the inline explanation is missing.

#### Minor (Nice to Have)

**`tests/conftest.py:146` ŌĆö `_reset_db_singletons` is `autouse=True` globally, running for every test including non-async unit tests.**

The fixture is an `Iterator[None]` (synchronous), so it does not cause errors in non-async tests, and the null assignments are essentially free. But future readers may wonder why the DB module is being mutated for config-only tests. Scoping it to the `integration` marker (or to `tests/test_db.py` and `tests/api/`) would be more precise. This is a cosmetic point ŌĆö the current form is correct and causes no failures.

**`src/fold_at_scripps/db.py:86ŌĆō89` ŌĆö `get_session` declared `async def` but typed as returning `AsyncIterator[AsyncSession]`, with no `@asynccontextmanager`.**

This is valid FastAPI generator-dependency syntax: an `async def` with `yield` implicitly creates an async generator. FastAPI handles it correctly. However, the return annotation `AsyncIterator[AsyncSession]` is technically imprecise ŌĆö the actual type is `AsyncGenerator[AsyncSession, None]`, which is a subtype of `AsyncIterator`. In practice this causes no runtime issue and mirrors the spec's verbatim code, so it is not a defect introduced by this implementer. Noting it for awareness only.

---

### Assessment

**Task quality: Approved ŌĆö fix the `# noqa` comment before merging.**

The implementation is correct, complete, and minimal. One Important finding: the `B008` suppression violates the project's explicit rule requiring an inline explanatory comment alongside the error code. The `_reset_db_singletons` deviation is justified, correctly diagnosed, and minimal. The Starlette deprecation warning is correctly attributed to the pre-existing `test_liveness` test using `TestClient`, not to this task's changes.</result>

input.description: Fix Task 4 B008 suppression finding
input.prompt:
You are applying a review fix to Task 4 (async DB + readiness) on branch `feature/foundation-scaffold` of the fold@Scripps FastAPI project. Work from: /home/briney/git/fold-at-scripps

## The finding to fix (Important)

In `src/fold_at_scripps/api/health.py`, the `readiness` endpoint carries `# noqa: B008` (ruff bugbear: function call `Depends(get_session)` in an argument default) without the explanatory comment the project rule requires. Rather than annotate the suppression (an inline comment would exceed the 100-char line limit, and every future endpoint with `Depends` would need its own `# noqa`), eliminate the suppression project-wide by telling ruff that FastAPI's dependency markers are immutable calls.

## Exact changes

1. In `pyproject.toml`, add a flake8-bugbear config section under the ruff lint config (alongside the existing `[tool.ruff.lint.isort]`). Add:

```toml
[tool.ruff.lint.flake8-bugbear]
extend-immutable-calls = [
    "fastapi.Depends",
    "fastapi.Query",
    "fastapi.Path",
    "fastapi.Header",
    "fastapi.Body",
    "fastapi.Form",
    "fastapi.File",
    "fastapi.Cookie",
    "fastapi.Security",
]
```

2. In `src/fold_at_scripps/api/health.py`, remove the trailing `  # noqa: B008` from the `readiness` function signature line so it reads:

```python
async def readiness(session: AsyncSession = Depends(get_session)) -> dict[str, str]:
```

Do not change any other behavior.

## Verify (covering checks for this change)

- `uv run ruff check .` ŌåÆ must report `All checks passed!` (confirms B008 no longer fires now that `Depends` is treated as immutable, and that no E501/other lint appears).
- `uv run ruff format --check .` ŌåÆ must pass.
- Ensure Postgres is up first: `docker compose up -d postgres` (wait for healthy). Then `uv run pytest tests/api/test_health.py -v` ŌåÆ both liveness and readiness tests must pass (behavior unchanged).

If `ruff check` still reports B008 after the config change, stop and report back what it says (the qualified-name matching may differ) rather than re-adding a noqa.

## Git

- Commit on the current branch `feature/foundation-scaffold` as a new commit (do not amend, do not push, do not create branches). Suggested message: `fix: allow FastAPI Depends in arg defaults via ruff config; drop B008 noqa`
- Stage only `pyproject.toml` and `src/fold_at_scripps/api/health.py`.

## Report

APPEND a "## Fix: B008 suppression" section to the existing report file /home/briney/git/fold-at-scripps/.superpowers/sdd/task-4-report.md containing: the changes made, the exact verify commands run and their output (ruff check, ruff format --check, the pytest result).

Then return ONLY (under 12 lines):
- **Status:** DONE | BLOCKED
- Commit created (short SHA + subject)
- One-line check summary (e.g. "ruff clean, format clean, 2/2 health tests pass")
- The report file path

No action required.

input.prompt:
You are implementing Task 5: Continuous integration.

## Task Description

Read your task brief first ŌĆö it is your requirements, with the exact workflow YAML to use verbatim:
/home/briney/git/fold-at-scripps/.superpowers/sdd/task-5-brief.md

## Context

This is the final task of the "Foundation & Scaffold" plan for **fold@Scripps**, a FastAPI front-end for the `autobio` biological-models package. Prior tasks (complete on this branch) produced: the `fold_at_scripps` package (config, main/app, api/health with `/health` and `/health/ready`, db engine), `pyproject.toml` (uv) with `uv.lock`, a `docker-compose.yml` (postgres on localhost:5432, db `fold_at_scripps`, user/pw `fold`/`fold`), and a test suite (sync unit tests + `integration`-marked tests that require Postgres).

This task adds a GitHub Actions workflow that runs ruff (check + format --check) and pytest against a Postgres service container, on push to main and on pull requests.

Note: the repo's `.gitignore` ignores `.github/hooks/` only ŌĆö `.github/workflows/` is tracked, so the workflow file will be committed normally.

Work from: /home/briney/git/fold-at-scripps

## Global Constraints

- `uv` for commands; package `fold_at_scripps`.
- The workflow must match the brief (service Postgres credentials `fold`/`fold`/`fold_at_scripps`, `FOLD_DATABASE_URL` env, ruff check + format --check + pytest steps).

## Interfaces

- Consumes: the `uv` project (`pyproject.toml`, `uv.lock`) and the full test suite including `integration`-marked tests (which need the Postgres service the workflow provides).
- Produces: `.github/workflows/ci.yml`.

## Git

- Commit on the current branch `feature/foundation-scaffold`. Do NOT create new branches and do NOT push.

## Before You Begin

If anything in the brief is unclear, ask now.

## Your Job

1. Create `.github/workflows/ci.yml` exactly as the brief specifies.
2. Step 2 of the brief validates the YAML. `pyyaml` is NOT a project dependency, so the `uv run python -c "import yaml..."` check will likely fail with ModuleNotFoundError ŌĆö that is expected. Per the brief's note, if it fails, instead verify the YAML by careful visual inspection of indentation/structure and say so in your report. Do not add pyyaml as a dependency.
3. Step 3: run the full local suite to confirm the branch is green before committing CI. Ensure Postgres is up first (`docker compose up -d postgres`, wait for healthy), then run `uv run ruff check . && uv run ruff format --check . && uv run pytest -v`. Report the result (expected: all checks pass, all tests pass ŌĆö 5 tests, 3 integration).
4. Commit as the brief's commit step directs (stage `.github/workflows/ci.yml`).
5. Self-review.
6. Report back.

## Report Format

Write your full report to /home/briney/git/fold-at-scripps/.superpowers/sdd/task-5-report.md, including: the workflow file created, how you validated the YAML (and the result of the pyyaml attempt), the full local suite output (ruff check, ruff format --check, pytest), self-review findings, any concerns.

Then return ONLY (under 15 lines):
- **Status:** DONE | DONE_WITH_CONCERNS | BLOCKED | NEEDS_CONTEXT
- Commits created (short SHA + subject)
- One-line summary (e.g. "CI workflow added; local suite 5/5 pass, ruff clean")
- Concerns, if any
- The report file path

If BLOCKED or NEEDS_CONTEXT, put the specifics in your final message.

This task adds a single GitHub Actions workflow file (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`); there is no application code. The diff's context lines ARE the changed file; do not Read it separately.
